feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?

sections.title: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA

paragraphs.text: EIMA
EUROPEAN
INFLUENCER
MARKETING
ALLIANCE

section_path: Digital Fairness Act
EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: The European Influencer Marketing Alliance (EIMA) is the federation of professional influencer marketing organizations across Europe. Our members currently represent 300 agencies and 8,000 creators through the leading trade bodies in 7 countries:

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: France (UMICC – Union des Métiers de l'Influence et des Créateurs de Contenu),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Germany (BVIM – Bundesverband Influencer Marketing),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: United Kingdom (IMTB – Influencer Marketing Trade Body),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Belgium (IMA – Influencer Marketing Association),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Spain (APIDE – Asociación Profesional de Influencers y Digitales de España),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Italy (AssoInfluencer),

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Poland (SPIM – Stowarzyszenie Polskiego Influencer Marketingu).

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: EIMA's growth follows the recommendations of the European Union Council's publication of conclusions on support for influencers as online content creators notably:

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Support emerging representative organisations to systematically raise awareness among influencers about their role in the media ecosystem and the impact they have on their audience [see para. 29];

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Encourage and support, in relevant ways, emerging representative organisations, influencer agencies, brands and other relevant actors in the development of self-regulatory bodies or mechanisms, including a possible ethical code or similar initiatives for influencers [see para 37];

section_path: EIMA: Who We Are?
paragraphs.text: Although national legal contexts differ, we share a common mission: to promote responsible, transparent, and fair influencer marketing in line with European consumer protection and digital policy objectives.

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Influencer marketing is now a core pillar of the digital economy, shaping consumer behavior, advertising standards, and digital platform dynamics:

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Influencer Marketing market value of 15 to 20B€ in 2025

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: More than 800,000 content creators in Europe (with >5k followers and at least 30% of their audience in one EU country. Source: Kolsquare)

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: 51% of European brands plan to increase their influencer marketing budget in 2025 (source: Kolsquare)

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: 24% of Italians said in 2023 that they had purchased a product because a celebrity or influencer advertised the product (source: Statista).

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: 87% of Gen Z consumers are more willing to buy from brands that partner with influencers beyond just social media content (source: Sprout Social)

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Yet, the sector remains very fragmented, with varying degrees of regulation and enforcement across Member States, which makes it difficult for stakeholders (creators, agents, agencies, technologies, and brands) operating in Europe to foster a sustainable, ethical, and profitable future for the influencer marketing industry.

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: EIMA is supportive of the objectives of the Digital Fairness Act expressly its desire to :

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Harmonize practices across Europe and reduce legal uncertainty for brands, agencies, agents, technologies, and creators.

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Protect consumers from unfair, misleading, or opaque digital practices while maintaining trust in creators.

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: Support SMBs and creators by ensuring a fairer, more transparent digital environment in which they can compete and thrive.

section_path: Why Influencer Marketing Matters for the DFA?
paragraphs.text: However, EIMA believes these objectives are achievable through building greater awareness of the existing legal framework with influencers, influencers marketers and advertisers and achieving higher compliance rates through education driven by industry actors including EIMA and the European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) rather than through the creation of additional legislation.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: Legal Framework already in Place:

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: a. EIMA welcomes the objectives of the DFA and believes the existing legal framework already covers influencer marketing practices effectively. For example:

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: i. Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) (Directive 2005/29/EC) where an influencer's hidden advertising is considered misleading according to Annex I No. 22 UCPD. The UCPD additionally provides strong tools to address deceptive practices and online fraud, including

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: fake likes and followers. This is supplemented by the Commission's UCPD guidance with a dedicated section on "online marketing".

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: ii. Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AMSD) (Directive 2010/13/EU) Art. 28b(2) in conjunction with Art. 9(1)(a) states that audiovisual commercial communication shall be readily recognisable as such.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: b. Before introducing new regulations the European Influencer Marketing Alliance (EIMA) would urge the Commission to evaluate the impact of recently adopted legislation and to work with self-regulators, and trade associations to build greater awareness of existing regulations, emphasising both the push and pull motivational drivers to adhere to existing regulations, together we should look to help advertisers, agencies and influencers acquire practical expertise in applying the regulations. In short - there is a need for increased enforcement and practical guidance rather than more regulation.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: c. EIMA would caution against the passing of new influencer marketing-specific laws. To do so would ghetto-ize our sector. Instead, all actors operating within the influencer marketing ecosystem should comply with existing advertising regulations like any other form of advertising.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: d. EIMA provides a practical way to share local expertise with partner members for the betterment of the influencer marketing industry, as well as a unified voice for creator economy matters to regulators, legislators, media, academics and other key stakeholders.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: e. The European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) has developed preventive tools to ensure responsible influencer marketing, such as adEthics, a training and certification programme for influencers, designed and implemented by national self-regulatory organisations.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: f. Statutory regulation has been augmented by advertising codes of conduct established by advertising associations and by self-regulatory organisations that cover digital media.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: Recognition of Influencer Marketing as a Distinct Sector: The DFA should acknowledge the specificity of influencer marketing as a hybrid space between advertising, digital platforms, and individual entrepreneurship. Influencers are neither platforms nor traditional media actors; without ghetto-izing creators, regulation, if it is created, must reflect this unique positioning.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: Strengthening Transparency and Disclosure Rules: Harmonized rules across the EU on commercial content disclosure (e.g., "ad") are essential. Currently, uneven national transpositions of the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD) create fragmentation and legal uncertainty. In addition, to simplify the application of commercial content disclosure, native advertising tags on platforms should be harmonized between platforms and aligned with the EU and local rules.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: **Fairness in Platform Relations**: Influencers, agents, and agencies often face imbalanced contractual relations with large digital platforms (algorithm changes, monetization policies, content moderation). The DFA should promote fair, transparent, and non-discriminatory terms, inspired by the logic of the Digital Markets Act but adapted to the creator economy.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: **Data Fairness and Access**: The influencer economy relies heavily on data (audience reach, engagement, conversion). Platforms must ensure fair access to accurate and reliable data for creators, agents, agencies, technologies, and advertisers. Without such access and transparency, SMBs and creators are at a disadvantage compared to large tech players.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: **Support for Responsible and Ethical Practices**: The European Commission should look to build awareness of the existing robust framework to enhance compliance by influencers, and for advertisers and agencies to understand their responsibilities. This should be achieved by working hand in hand with EIMA, self-regulatory organisations and EASA. Additionally, the European Commission he DFA should encourage self-regulation and co-regulation initiatives led by professional organizations. EIMA and its members already promote codes of ethics, educational initiatives, and enforcement mechanisms. EU-level recognition of these efforts would foster credibility and uptake.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: a. EIMA believes there is an opportunity for regulators, SROs, trade associations and social media platforms to work in concert to create a wider and deeper sweep of influencer content in terms of numbers of influencers analysed, platforms content published to and the verticals in which they are published. The exercise would build on the 2023 sweep of 576 influencers and would chart progress towards acceptable disclosure levels.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: b. The excellent work undertaken on the Influencer Legal Hub might be built upon to create bite sized content delivered in the style and form most likely to be absorbed by influencers. Social media platforms might be encouraged to promote the Influencer Legal Hub to influencers using its platform.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: EIMA and its members stand ready to serve as a trusted partner for EU institutions in shaping the Digital Fairness Act. Influencer marketing is at the intersection of digital platforms, consumer protection, and entrepreneurship. To succeed, the DFA must not overlook this rapidly growing sector.

section_path: EIMA's Key Comments and Recommendations for DFA
paragraphs.text: We encourage the European Commission to consider the unique challenges and opportunities of influencer marketing in its legislative process and invite further dialogue with our alliance.
